Summary: FOXA1 functions as a pioneer factor in luminal prostate epithelium and in prostate cancer. FOXA1 mutations, reported in Asian populations, alter its pioneering activity and perturb luminal epithelial differentiation, prompting prostate cancer progression. Ivermectin disturbed the pioneering function of FOXA1 and decreased chromatin accessibility, suppressing AR signaling. It also blocks FOXA1 and Ku70/Ku80, which results in suppression of E2F1 expression and deficiency of DNA double-strand break (DSB) repair. Ivermectin downregulates MET, MMP7, and SOX9 and does not induce EMT. Reduced repair capacity contributes to G0/G1 arrest and synthetic lethality. Ivermectin induces G0/G1 arrest, which is associated with suppression of prostate cancer progression. Ivermectin triggers synthetic lethality, which is associated with suppression of prostate cancer progression. Targeting FOXA1 is an important strategy for castration‑resistant prostate cancer and ivermectin merits further development.
Reference: Lv, S., Wu, Z., Luo, M., Zhang, Y., Zhang, J., Pascal, L. E., Wang, Z., & Wei, Q. (2022). Integrated analysis reveals FOXA1 and Ku70/Ku80 as targets of ivermectin in prostate cancer. Cell Death Dis, 13(9), 754. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41419-022-05182-0

Summary: Systematic: In prostate cancer, FOXA1 functions as the primary orchestrator of alternative splicing dysregulation across 500 primary and metastatic transcriptomes. FOXA1 binds to the regulatory regions of splicing-related genes including HNRNPK and SRSF1. By controlling trans-acting factor expression, FOXA1 exploits an "exon definition" mechanism that calibrates alternative splicing toward dominant isoform production and reduces nonsense-mediated decay–targeted isoforms. The FOXA1-controlled oncogene SRSF1 promotes inclusion of the NMD-determinant FLNA exon 30, and that inclusion promotes cell growth in vitro and predicts disease recurrence. FOXA1 thus rewires splicing from chromatin access to splicing-factor regulation to exon changes that influence patient survival.
Reference: Del Giudice, M., Foster, J. G., Peirone, S., Rissone, A., Caizzi, L., Gaudino, F., Parlato, C., Anselmi, F., Arkell, R., Guarrera, S., Oliviero, S., Basso, G., Rajan, P., & Cereda, M. (2022). FOXA1 regulates alternative splicing in prostate cancer. Cell Rep, 40(13), 111404. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.celrep.2022.111404

Summary: FOXA1 is a pioneering transcription factor that targets androgen receptor (AR) and has a demonstrated role in prostate cancer oncogenesis. In primary prostate cancers FOXA1 is mutated in a minority of tumors and those mutations are mutually exclusive with other genomic subclass–defining alterations. A subset of FOXA1‑mutant tumors also harbored SPOP mutations and exhibited elevated AR output. Most FOXA1 mutations were missense and primarily affected the winged‑helix DNA‑binding domain. These mutations did not directly alter DNA‑contacting residues and thus suggest disruption of interactions with other chromatin‑bound cofactors rather than altered DNA binding. Mutations in FOXA1 defined a major molecular subtype of primary prostate cancers, and FOXA1‑mutant tumors had the highest levels of AR‑induced transcripts among subtypes.
Reference: Cancer, G. e. n. o. m. e. A. t. l. a. s. R. e. s. e. a. r. c. h. N. e. t. w. o. r. k. (2015). The Molecular Taxonomy of Primary Prostate Cancer. Cell, 163(4), 1011-1025. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cell.2015.10.025

Summary: Systematic elaboration. FOXA1 is recurrently mutated across prostate cancer stages, with Forkhead Wing2 coding mutations emerging in localized disease and truncation mutations and structural variants enriched in metastatic disease. Reported mutation frequencies reach up to 9% in primary prostate cancer, up to 13% in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer, about 25% in a 29-sample neuroendocrine cohort, and 41% of localized tumors in the Chinese cohort. Germline SNPs and somatic SNVs modulate FOXA1 genomic binding and transcriptional regulation by altering FOXA1 binding at cis-regulatory elements, with germline SNPs producing allele-specific binding of AR-FOXA1 complexes. For example, rs1859961 reduces FOXA1 binding at a SOX9-driving CRE. rs183373024 abolishes binding at 8q24 and a subset of somatic SNVs increases FOXA1 binding at CREs regulating MYC and FOXA1. FOXA1 3′ UTR indels dominate 3′ UTR alterations among driver genes in plasma cell-free DNA from mCRPC patients and can appear early in prostate tumours while being absent from other cancer types. Normal tissues should not exhibit FOXA1 mutations, so including FOXA1 in targeted sequencing of urine or blood permits liquid biopsy detection of minimal residual disease and longitudinal monitoring for recurrence. The mutation spectrum can inform progression and warrants evaluation for prognostic use and mutant-specific inhibitors.
Reference: Teng, M., Zhou, S., Cai, C., Lupien, M., & He, H. H. (2021). Pioneer of prostate cancer: past, present and the future of FOXA1. Protein Cell, 12(1), 29-38. https://doi.org/10.1007/s13238-020-00786-8

Summary: Whole-exome sequencing of seven human prostate cancer organoid lines derived from biopsy specimens and circulating tumor cells identified disease-specific FOXA1 mutations. FOXA1 mutations are listed among genetic lesions that underlie prostate tumorigenesis, and FOXA1-mutant tumors are not represented in the limited available prostate cancer cell lines.
Reference: Gao, D., Vela, I., Sboner, A., Iaquinta, P. J., Karthaus, W. R., Gopalan, A., Dowling, C., Wanjala, J. N., Undvall, E. A., Arora, V.
